SpTU 3, 106 [Šammu Šikinšu][via cams/gkab]

Obverse
oo NaN  (start of obverse missing)
o 1'o 1'

[...] x SU x [...]

(o 1') (traces)

o 2'2'

[...] ⸢TI⸣ šimMA x [...]

(o 2') [...] ...-aromatic substance ... [...]

o 3'3'

[...] ⸢GIM⸣ u₂duḫ-ni [...]

(o 3') [...] is like millet [...]. It is good for ears which continually contain pus. ... [...].

o 4'4'

ana GEŠTU-MIN ša₂ LUGUD ŠUBa SIG₅ x [...]

o 5'5'

[...] GAR-šu₂ GIM gišU₃.SUḪ₅ ti-ri-in-na-ti-[šu₂ ...]

(o 5') [...] whose appearance is like a pine-tree. Its cones [...].

o 6'6'

U₂ GAR-šu₂ GIM ZA₃.ḪI.LI NUMUN-šu₂ <GIM> NUMUN ZA₃.ḪI.LI [...]

(o 6') The plant whose appearance is like cress. Its seeds are like cress seeds [...] is good for (putting) the belly in good condition. You pound (it). In ... [...].

o 7'7'

ana ŠA₃ SI.SA₂ SIG₅ SUD₂ ina x [...]

o 8'8'

U₂ GAR-šu₂ GIM u₂-paṭ!(IGI) u₂KIŠI₁₆ NUMUN-šu₂ GIM NUMUN ḪI.ISsar U₂ mat-qu MU-⸢NI⸣ [...]

(o 8') The plant whose appearance is like exudation of ašāgu-plant. Its seeds are like lettuce seeds. Its name is sweet plant. [...].

o 9'9'

U₂ GAR-šu₂ GIM <u₂>UKUŠ₂.LA₂ E₃-su ru-uš-šat u₂IGI-lim MU-NI ana GIG ⸢DU₃⸣.[A.BI ...]

(o 9') The plant whose appearance is like cucumber. Its growth is red. Its name is "imhur-lim". [...] for all illnesses.

o 10'10'

U₂ GAR-šu₂ GIM U₂.dUTU NUMUN-šu₂ GIM ši-gu-uš-ti u₂IGI.20 [...]

(o 10') The plant whose appearance is like sunflower. Its seeds are like šigguštu-plant. imhur-ešrā [...]. It is good for long-lasting illnesses. You dry, you pound (and) [...] in front of the patient.

o 11'11'

ana GIG la-az-zi SIG₅ ḪAD₂.DU SUD₂ ana IGI ⸢GIG⸣ [...]

o 12'12'

U₂ GAR-šu₂ GIM u₂ti-ig-il-le-e NUMUN-šu₂ GIM ši-gu-uš-ti u₂AŠ.TAL₂.TAL₂ [...]

(o 12') The plant whose appearance is like colocynth. Its seeds are like šigguštu-plant. [...] ardadillu-plant.

o 13'13'

U₂ GAR-šu₂ GIM ši-kin za-ap-pi x-da SA₅ u₂el-kul-la MU-NI ana IGI-MIN ša₂ x [...]

(o 13') The plant whose appearance is like the form of a bristle. It is full of .... Its name is "elkulla". [...] for the eyes that ... [...].

o 14'14'

U₂ GAR-šu₂ GIM ši-kin giš-gir-ri GE₆ u₃ UGU KU ZU u₂x [...]

(o 14') The plant whose appearance is like black wooden spill and ... [...]. It is good for eradication of affliction of a ghost [...].

o 15'15'

ana DAB GIDIM ZI-šu₂ ⸢SIG₅⸣ [...]

o 16'16'

U₂ GAR-šu₂ GIM ši-kin gišḪAŠḪUR ina ni-siḫ₂ A.AB.BA KI U₂ u GI [...]

(o 16') The plant whose appearance is like the form of a hašhūru-plant. [...] from extraction of the sea where plant and reed [...] do not exist.

o 17'17'

la₃ GAL₂-MEŠ ina IGI A-MEŠ E₃ ina muḫ-ḫi-⸢šu₂⸣ [...]

(o 17') It emerges on the surface of the water. [...] on top of it.

o 18'18'

u₂LAL MU-NI ana GIG šu-ru-pe-e TAB.U₄.DA u ⸢UŠ₁₁⸣ [...]

(o 18') Its name is ašqulālu-plant. [...] for illness of frost, heat stroke and sorcery.

o 19'19'

U₂ GAR-šu₂ GIM ši-kin u₂EME.UR.GI₇ MIN U₂ GAR-šu₂ GIM u₂LU.UB₂sar u₃ [...]

(o 19') The plant whose appearance is like the form of hound's-tongue plant. Alternatively, the plant whose appearance is like turnip and continually seeks [...]. It grows in an irrigated field. Wherever you pull it out, its root [...]

o 20'20'

iš-te-ne₂-ʾe ina A.ŠA₃ mi-ik-ri E₃ e-ma ZI-šu₂ šu-ru-⸢us⸣-[su ...]

o 21'21'

i-kap-pap u₂lid-da-[na-nu] ḫi-pi₂ eš-šu₂x [...]

(o 21') it curves. liddanānu-plant (gloss: new break) ... [...].

o 22'22'

U₂ GAR-šu₂ GIM u₂LU.UB₂sar NUMUN-šu₂ GIM NUMUN u₂ŠE.MUŠ₅sar u₂SES x ḫi-pi₂NUMUN-šu₂ ana ŠA₃ SI.SA₂ SIG₅ ⸢SUD₂⸣ [...]; ina A-MEŠ la₃ pa-tan NAG!(GU₇)

(o 22') The plant whose appearance is like turnip. Its seeds are like seed of šigūšu-barley. Myrrh ... (gloss: break). Its seeds are good for putting the belly in good condition. You pound [...]. He drinks (it) in water without dining.

Reverse
r 1r 1

u₂LAG.A.ŠA₃.GA u₂DUR₂.[GIG] ḫi-pi₂ eš-⸢šu₂⸣[...]

(r 1) Camomile, ašdānu-plant (gloss: new break) [...]

r 22

U₂ pap-pa!(SA-)a-nu SIG₂-MEŠ IL₂ u₂ka-da-nu MU-NI ḫi-pi₂ eš-šu₂tu-pa-ṣa ana DUR₂-šu₂ GAR[an ...]

(r 2) A rush-plant that bears wool: Its name is kadānu-plant. (gloss: new break). You crush. You put (the plant) into his anus. [...].

r 33

u₂tim-bu-ut-ti₃ A.ŠA₃ u₂<<A>>.DUR₂.GIG ina I₃.UDU ḪI.ḪI ana DUR₂-šu₂ GAR⸢an⸣

(r 3) You mix field-drum-plant (and) ašdānu-plant in fat. You put (the treatment) into his anus.

r 44

u₂šimSES <<GAR>> šimLI!(SAR) U₂ 2 ina I₃.UDU ḪI.ḪI il-la-nu DU₃uš ana DUR₂-šu₂ GAR[an]

(r 4) You mix myrrh (and) juniper, the 2 plants, in fat. You make a acorn-shaped suppository. You put it into his anus.

r 55

u₂šimSES U₂ ša₂-aš₂-ša₂-ṭi ina KAŠ SEG₆.GA₂ x [...]

(r 5) You cook myrrh and plant against šaššaṭu-disease in beer. ... [...].

r 66

u₂MA? u₂KAL SIG₇ MU-NI u₂bu-uʾ-⸢ša₂-nu⸣ x x x x [...]

(r 6) ...-plant: yellow-green ...-plant is its name. būšānu-plant ... [...].

r 77

u₂KAL? u₂ṣa-ṣu-un-tu₂ MU-NI u₂x [...]

(r 7) ...-plant: šašuntu-plant is its name. ...-plant [...].

r 88

u₂ḪAB? u₂ŠINIG ⸢TI?⸣ KU₅si [...]

(r 8) būšānu-plant, tamarisk ... cutting [...].

r 99

u₂ŠAKIR u₂su-pa-lu [...]

(r 9) Henbane, supālu-juniper [...].

r 1010

u₂su-pa-lu u₂kam-[man-tu₄ ...]

(r 10) supālu-juniper, kamantu-plant [...]

r 1111
